symbols: infant Christ reaching toward Catherine — the mystical union between Christ and the faithful soul
intimate clustering of figures — the communion of saints gathered around the incarnate Word
Mary presenting the child — the Mother of God as mediatrix, presenting Christ to the devoted
description: A warmly toned painting showing four figures in an intimate close grouping. At center, a nude infant Christ leans forward with a joyful expression, reaching toward a young woman with curly hair on the right who looks outward at the viewer — St. Catherine. Behind the child, the Virgin Mary gazes tenderly at the scene. At the upper left, an elderly bearded man, likely Joseph, looks on from the shadows. The composition is tightly cropped, emphasizing the physical and emotional closeness of the figures. Warm golden and reddish tones dominate.
theological_description: The Mystic Marriage of St. Catherine is a popular devotional subject depicting the legendary vision in which the infant Christ placed a ring on Catherine's finger, signifying her spiritual betrothal to Him. While Catherine of Alexandria is a post-biblical saint, the theology of mystical marriage between Christ and the believing soul is deeply rooted in Scripture. Paul writes in 2 Corinthians 11:2, 'I promised you to one husband, to Christ, so that I might present you as a pure virgin to Him.' The Song of Solomon has been interpreted throughout Christian tradition as an allegory of Christ's love for the Church and for the individual soul (Song 2:16, 'My beloved is mine and I am his'). Ephesians 5:25-32 develops this marriage metaphor extensively, calling the union of Christ and the Church a profound mystery. The infant Christ's joyful reaching toward Catherine visualizes the divine initiative in love — it is God who first loved us (1 John 4:19). Mary's mediating role in presenting Christ reflects the Incarnation through which God became accessible to human touch and intimacy.

scripture_refs.reason: Paul's image of presenting believers as a pure virgin betrothed to Christ is the theological basis for mystical marriage imagery
The marriage of Christ and the Church as a profound mystery underlies the mystical marriage tradition
The intimate language of the Song of Solomon — my beloved is mine — interpreted as Christ's love for the soul
The infant Christ reaching first toward Catherine embodies the truth that we love because He first loved us
